observations.recommendation:
Based on the foregoing, we recommended the Municipal Mayor to:

a. prioritize the conduct of one-time cleansing of PPE accounts as soon as possible by creating an Inventory Committee composed of adequate number of members to be able to complete the physical inventory in three months or less;

b. instruct the newly created Inventory Committee, GSO and the Accounting Unit to perform the preliminary activities through preparation of required reports such as latest RPCPPE, Inventory Count Form and PPELCs and reconcile their records before the actual physical inventory; and

c. cause the preparation of PIP containing, at the least, the specific assignments/duties of the Committee members, the cut-off date and a schedule specifying the dates and locations of the inventory taking activities from start up to the targeted completion of the physical inventory to be submitted to the Audit Team at least ten (10) calendar days before the scheduled inventory taking activities.

We recommended that the Municipal Mayor instruct the Municipal Accountant to:

a. conduct final confirmation among identified source agencies to confirm the balances due to them and facilitate the return of the remaining unutilized/unused fund. Otherwise, these balances could be processed for the one-time cleansing of receivable and other fund transfers as provided under COA Circular No. 2023-008 dated August 17, 2023; and

b. ensure that all subsequent unused balance of funds received shall be returned immediately after the completion of the project pursuant to COA Circular No. 94-013.

We strongly reiterated that the Municipal Mayor direct the Municipal Accountant to initiate the request for write-off of the long outstanding balances following the guidelines and procedures prescribed under the aforementioned COA Circular.

We recommended that the Municipal Mayor instruct the Municipal Accountant to:

a. reconcile employees’ records at GSIS, PhilHealth and Pag-IBIG to identify the affected employees on the unremitted balances and immediately remit the amounts due to these agencies and identify the cause/s of the over remittance for some accounts and make the proper adjustments to avoid the incurrence of unnecessary financial expenses in the form of imposed penalties; and

b. review the taxes withheld from employees, suppliers and contractors and ensure that these taxes are remitted on the prescribed period to avoid penalties and surcharges.

We recommended that the Municipal Mayor require the Municipal Accountant to review and reconcile the Subsidy from Other Funds and Subsidy to Other Funds accounts presented in the financial statements as of year-end pursuant to Paragraph 27 of IPSAS 1 and COA Circular No. 2015-009.

We recommended that the Municipal Mayor as the Chairperson of the Executive Committee together with the Gender Focal Point System and the Technical Working Group to:

a. exert active efforts in accomplishing all the targeted and planned client-focused and organization-focused PPAs;

b. establish and maintain GAD database; and

c. coordinate with the Municipal Human Resources Management Officer to require all members of the GFPS including elected local officials and department heads to undergo capacity building programs on GAD which shall include but not be limited to: gender sensitivity training (GST), gender-responsive planning and budgeting, gender analysis, gender audit and GAD tools such as the Gender Mainstreaming Evaluation Framework (GMEF), Gender Responsive LGU Ka Ba (GeRL Ka Ba) Self-Assessment Tool, Harmonized Gender and Development Guidelines (HGDG) and other succeeding GAD tools.

We recommended that the Municipal Mayor require the GAD Focal Point System to prepare the GAD Agenda in accordance with Section 4.C.2, paragraph 1 of PCW-DILG-DBM-NEDA Joint Memorandum Circular (JMC) No. 2013-01 and Item 5, paragraph 6 of PCW-DILG-DBM-NEDA JMC No. 2016-01.

We recommended that the Municipal Mayor require the concerned officials/departments to indicate the brand names/model and complete detailed specifications, in the succeeding Purchase Orders, to be prepared by the Municipality.

Likewise, instruct the Inspection Committee and General Services Officer to submit explanation/justification on deficiencies noted as discussed in Item nos. 2-4 above.
Moreso, require delivery from the respective suppliers of the items agreed upon per Purchase Order in Project no. 1 (Item nos. 4 and 5), Project no. 2 (Item no. 11), and Project no. 4 (Item nos. 1, 4 and 6).

Finally, ensure the installation of licensed antivirus software and Microsoft Office on all the procured desktop computers and laptops.

We recommended that the Municipal Mayor direct the:

a. Municipal Accountant to maintain Subsidiary Ledger for Cash-Local Treasury for each Collecting Officer and update them daily;

b. Municipal Treasurer to require all Collecting Officers to maintain Cashbook to record their collections and remittances daily; and

c. Municipal Treasurer to ensure that all collections are duly acknowledged through affixing the signature of proper AO on the AFs.

Likewise, we recommended that the Municipal Mayor refrain from designating functions of collecting and disbursing to the same person to promote proper segregation of duties for a sound internal control system on cash management.

We recommended that the Municipal Mayor direct the BAC to require future contract awardees for the submission of performance bond with proper period of validity pursuant to Section 39.3 of the Revised IRR of RA No. 9184.

We recommended that the Municipal Mayor direct the Municipal Engineer to regularly conduct actual inspection of the project for any damage within the one-year defects liability period and accordingly recommend the issuance of Certificate of Final Acceptance for completed Infrastructure Projects after such period.

We recommended that the Municipal Mayor direct the:

a. Municipal Engineer to require the contractor to post warranty securities for Structural Defects and Failures after the one-year defects liability period for the project, Construction/Improvement of Alitagtag Business and Recreation Center at Poblacion, Alitagtag, Batangas with the correct period;

b. Municipal Engineer to ensure that recent and succeeding Infrastructure Projects will be monitored for issuance of Certificate of Final Acceptance and posting of warranty securities in the form prescribed in the Section 62.2.3.3 of the Revised IRR of RA No. 9184; and

c. General Services Officer to require suppliers to post warranty securities for manufacturing defects after acceptance by the Municipality of delivery for procurement of Goods in the form prescribed in Section 62.1 of the Revised IRR of RA No. 9184.
We recommended Municipal Mayor require submission of documentary requirements to ascertain claims of ownership and compliance with COA Circular No. 2012-001 and PD No. 1445.

observations.recommendation:
We recommended that the Municipal Mayor direct the GSO Designate and the
Municipal Accountant to reclassify the 425 semi-expendable items totaling
P1,621,545.52 to appropriate inventory accounts and ensure that all small value
assets with useful life of more than one year but costing less than P15,000.00 are
properly classified and recorded accordingly and the Municipal Accountant to
prepare journal entries and effect the necessary adjustments on the PPE,
Accumulated Depreciation and Government Equity accounts for a fair presentation
of the accounts in the financial statements.

We further recommended that the Municipality follow the guidelines set forth in
COA Circular No. 2020-006 for the one-time cleansing of PPE accounts.

We recommended that the Municipal Mayor direct the:

a. Bids and Awards Committee (BAC) to:
· provide an explanation for the non-posting of Notice of Award, Notice to
Proceed, and approved Contract in the PhilGEPS;
· submit a written justification why the submitted Tax Clearance Certificates
of Michaevelli Construction and Supplies were not validated during Bid
evaluation and Post-Qualification process, which resulted in the award of
contract to ineligible bidder. Enforce strictly the sanctions against the erring
contractor for submitting spurious Tax Clearance Certificates (TCCs); the BAC;
and BAC TWG for not conducting bid evaluation and post-qualification in
accordance with Section 34, Rule X of the 2016 Revised IRR of RA No. 9184;
· submit immediately all the noted lacking documents enumerated in Annex
K to the Office of the Auditor for verification and audit.

b. Municipal Accountant to ensure the completeness of the supporting
documents as required under Section 4(6) of PD No. 1445 and Section 9.1 of
COA Circular No. 2012-001 before processing payments to contractors; and

Henceforth, comply strictly with Sections 23, 34.3, 37.1.6 and 37.4.2 of the 2016
Revised IRR of RA No. 9184, Section 4(6) of PD No. 1445 and Section 9.1 of
COA Circular No. 2012-001.

We recommended that the Municipal Mayor direct the:

a. Municipal Accountant to:
· effect in the books the necessary adjustments to record the reconciling items
totalling to P39,210.50 duly supported with valid documents to reflect the
correct cash balance in the financial statements;
· adjust in the books the stale checks amounting to P18,232.49; and
· make proper coordination with the concerned bank Officials to adjust the
noted recurring bank reconciling items;

b. Municipal Treasurer to cancel the unclaimed stale checks and furnish the
Municipal Accountant with the original Disbursement Vouchers (DV) and its
supporting documents for preparation of the JEV following the procedures
prescribed to ensure recording of such cancellation.

We recommended and the management agreed that the Municipal Mayor require the
Municipal Accountant and the Municipal Budget Officer to use the proper accounts in
recording transaction and ensure that all expenditures of the municipality are
properly classified in accordance with the revised chart of accounts prescribed in
COA Circular No. 2015-009.

We recommended that the Municipal Mayor instruct the Municipal Accountant to
record subsequent procurement of goods, supplies and materials as an asset under
appropriate Inventory account upon purchase, and to recognize its related expenses
only when supported with the Report of Supplies and Materials Issued from the end-
users and Supply Officer with attached pre-numbered Requisition and Issue Slips.

We recommended that the Municipal Mayor direct the:

a. Municipal Accountant to:
· ensure that cash advances be granted only for specific purpose and for
expenses which are difficult, impractical or impossible to be paid by check
aside from ensuring that the government treasury received the mandatory
collections from business enterprises; and
· ensure that liquidation of cash advances are supported with complete
documents for ensuing transactions. Thereafter, check the appropriateness of
the documents attached in the liquidation reports.

b. Accountable Officers to submit a written explanation on the noted documentary
deficiencies.

We also recommended that the Management submit a written justification of the
necessity of paying the transactions in cash through a cash advance instead through
the issuance of check.

We recommended and the Management agreed that the Municipal Mayor require the
Human Resource Management Officer, Municipal Treasurer and Municipal Accountant
to control the accommodation given to financing institutions and ensure that the
minimum monthly net take home pay of all officials and employees shall not be lower
than Five Thousand Pesos (P5,000.00) pursuant to the General Appropriations Act.

We recommended that the Municipal Mayor direct the Municipal School Board
(MSB) and concerned Officials to submit justification on the noted observations and to
refrain from charging non-priority expenses to SEF.

We recommended that the Municipal Disaster Risk Reduction and Mitigation Office
(MDRRMO) monitor and evaluate the utilization of LDRRMF based on the programs,
projects and activities listed in the Local Disaster Risk Reduction and Mitigation Fund
Investment Plan (LDRRMFIP) to ensure the full implementation of the pre-disaster
preparedness and response activities of the Municipality.

We also recommended strict compliance to Sections 2(e) and 12(6) of RA No. 10121 to
strengthen the capacity of the Municipality to build a disaster resilient community.

We recommended that the MDRRM Council decide on the use of the unexpended
balance of the LDRRMF, and review and update annually the LDRRMF Investment
Plan to incorporate the PPAs to be funded by the unexpended QRF and DRRMF-
MOOE of the previous years so that the Municipality becomes a disaster resilient
community.

Henceforth, strictly adhere to Sections 5.1.2 and 5.1.15 of COA Circular No. 2012-002.

We recommended and the Management agreed that the Municipal Mayor direct the
Municipal Accountant to make the necessary journal entry to present the correct year-
end balances of the affected accounts in the financial statements of the Municipality.
Henceforth, record under the Trust Liabilities-DRRMF account the DRRM funds
received from National Government.

a. Certification and/or documents that will show that said savings in the amount of

procedures and cost-cutting measures;
b. Report/Summary/Schedule showing percentage (%) of accomplishments against
targeted programs/activities/projects as prescribed in Item 4.1.2 (Accomplishment

c. The Employees' Organization-Management Consultative Committee’s written

d. Written justification for the immediate payment of CNA Incentive after the
approval and signing of CNA; and
e. Henceforth, identify in the CNA the cost-cutting measures and system
improvement that the management and the association have to undertake in order
128 to meet and deliver the planned target, programs or services at a lesser cost.

We also recommended that the Municipal Accountant draw the necessary adjusting
entry to correct the recording of payment of the CNA Incentive pursuant to Item 4.4.4
of DBM Circular No. 2020-5.

We further recommended that in future similar transaction, the required supporting
documents are already attached before payment of CNA Incentive pursuant to DBM
Budget Circular No. 2020-5, to avoid audit suspensions and disallowances.

Management’s comment/s:
On June 10, 2021, the Municipality submitted the following documents in compliance to the
above cited observations:
1. Certification signed by the Municipal Mayor certifying that the amount of
P1,350,000.00 were generated out of improvement/streamlining of LGU systems
and procedures and employment of cost-cutting measures in the implementation
of LGU planned targets, programs and services in the approved budget. Also
stated therein that neither of the projects nor activities of the LGU were sacrificed
due to the generation of abovementioned savings;
2. Accomplishment Report on Targeted Programs/Projects/Activities approved in
the Annual Budget for CY 2020 (as of September 30, 2020);
3. Copy of AMEA Resolution No. 01-2020 dated November 17, 2020 approved by
the Municipal Mayor adopting the guidelines on the grant of CNA Incentives to
the employees of the Municipal Government of Alitagtag, Batangas.
Moreover, the Municipal Mayor commented in his letter dated June 01, 2021 and received
by the Office of the Auditor on June 10, 2021 that they admitted the error in recording the
CNA incentive in the books of account and that the Municipal Accountant will make the
necessary correcting entries for the error. He also assured that this error will not occur again
on their future transaction.

He also explained the payment of the CNA incentive after the approval and signing of CNA,
quoted as follows:
We also admit that the CNA was granted after a month that it was signed.
However, it is our humble opinion that the incentive granted cannot be considered
as signing bonus in character because the incentive was granted only after
satisfaction of the requirements set by DBM Budget Circular No. 2020-5 wherein
the LGU has accomplished at least an average of 70% of all targets for all the
organizational outcomes/performances indicators under our respective FY 2020
budget approved by the Sangguniang Bayan as of September 30, 2020 and
savings from Maintenance and Other Operating Expenses were generated from
the planned programs, projects and activities under the CNA and NOT as a pre-
conditioned or as an incentive to lure the signing and passing of the CNA.
The primary purpose of the grant of the incentive to the Municipal Officials and
employees is to recognize the efforts of employees and management of the
Municipal Government in the achievement of planned targets, programs and
services approved in the budgets at lesser cost. Our clear objective is to
encourage, promote and reward productivity, efficiency and use of austerity
measures as specified in the CNA.
Lastly, the COVID-19 pandemic caused so much distress, not only in health and
financial status of the people. The incentive granted greatly helped our officials and
employees during those period thereby uplifting their morale and productivity.”

Auditor’s Rejoinder:
We would like to invite the attention of the Municipality on the following provisions of the
Public Sector Labor-Management Council (PSLMC) Resolution No. 04, s. 2002 dated
November 14, 2002 re: Grant of Collective Negotiation Agreement (CNA) Incentive for
National Government Agencies, State Universities and Colleges and Local Government
Units, viz:
"Section 1. In recognition of the joint efforts of labor and management to achieve
all planned targets, programs and services approved in the budget of the agency at
a lesser cost, CNA Incentive may be provided in the CNA. To ensure that funds are
available and still all planned targets, programs and services approved in the
budget of the agency are achieved, only savings generated after the signing of
the CNA may be used for the CNA Incentive.
Section 2. To ensure that savings will be generated after the signing, the CNA
must include provisions on cost cutting measures and systems improvement
that will be undertaken by both the management and the union so that the
delivery of service or achievement of agency targets can be made at less
cost. (Emphasis ours)"
The incentive shall be sourced solely from savings generated as a result of the cost-cutting
measures/systems improvement agreed upon by the union/association and the agency
management. Hence, the incentive cannot be given upon signing of the agreement by the
parties, otherwise, it will partake of the nature of a signing bonus.
As a final point, the Audit Team would like to reiterate that the cost-cutting measures and
system improvement that the management and the association would have to undertake in
order to meet and deliver the planned target, programs or services at a lesser cost should
be specifically identified in the CNA.

We recommended that the Municipal Mayor direct the Municipal Treasurer to adopt additional measures and strategies to intensify collection of taxes and generate more income; coordinate with the barangays in the implementation of collection of taxes and implement the Real Property Tax Program; coordinate with the Provincial Government and Sangguniang Panlalawigan to avail of remedies and sanctions as provided in RA No. 7160 to deter delay and non-payment of taxes.
observations.managementAction: The management is already undertaking coordination with the 19 barangays in the locality in full implementation of the agency’s action plan for the implementatio n of the Real Property Tax Program. observations.recommendation: We recommended that the Municipal Mayor prioritize the completion of physical count of the Municipal properties by: a. designating additional personnel under the supervision of the GSO to conduct the complete inventory; b. requiring the designated GSO and the Municipal Accountant to immediately reconcile the results of inventory per RPCPPE; c. compelling the use of stock cards in the General Service Office to facilitate better monitoring and reconciliation; d. requiring deeds of donation to be executed to transfer barangay structures; e. exert more effort to locate the recorded PPE without records in the books; f. verify and validate the 11 parcels of land and provide funding for titling under the name of the Municipality for recording in the book of accounts.

observations.recommendation: We recommended that the Municipal Mayor direct the Municipal Accountant to: a. observations.recommendation: We recommended that the Management: a. observations.managementAction: A written justification/explanation was submitted to the Audit Team on December 22, 2020. Lacking documents were submitted to the Audit Team for review and evaluation. Austerity measures are being observed in the Municipality.

observations.recommendation: We recommended that the Municipality be cautious in the utilization of its Special Education Fund. Provide funding from the general fund for the total of P268,980.00. Refrain from charging non-priority expenses against the fund to avoid disallowance in audit.
observations.managementAction: The Municipality was not able to process the purchase for CY 2020 due to lack of funds and compliance with the Joint Circular. No further disbursement was made.

observations.recommendation: We recommended that the Municipal Mayor and the Sangguniang Bayan exercise the prerogative to revise and update the Local Revenue Code (LRC) pursuant to Sections 129 and 191 of RA No. 7160 to increase the revenue collection to meet the financial needs of the Municipality.
observations.managementAction: The Municipality is still studying the revision of the LRC.

observations.recommendation: We recommended that the Municipal Mayor and Management: a. The MDRRM Council decide on the use of the unexpended balance of the LDRRMF, review and update annually the LDRRMF Investment Plan; d. observations.reason: Utilization of the mitigation fund was 71.93% during the year; some recommendations are being implemented but not fully; verification and analysis ongoing.

observations.reason: Submission was made but some supporting items including Certificate of Review and Endorsement by DILG Province of Batangas were not furnished.

observations.recommendation:

conduct monitoring and evaluation of these account and cause the immediate

2016-005 dated December 19, 2016.

damages or losses caused by fire, earthquake and other covered calamities.

The Management said that will look for funding source after a complete inventory was
conducted to ensure the amount of properties that need insurance coverage.

observations.managementAction: The LDRRMO is now closely monitoring the implementation of all projects funded by the LDRRM and complied with the recommendation on preparation of the LDRRMFIP. The LDRRMO already complied with the recommendation.
observations.reason: The LDRRMO complied with the recommendation.

observations.recommendation: We recommend that the Municipal Mayor require the Municipal Development Council to: a) require the continuation of the implementation of priority developmental projects funded by 20% Development Fund and consider other feasible priority projects for the reversion of appropriated funds for projects not implemented to ensure that the full intention of development will benefit all its constituents; b) impose liquidated damages to be deducted from any amount still due to the contractor; and c) instruct the Bids and Awards Committee (BAC) to validate the contractor’s list of on-going projects/awarded not yet started; coordinate/confirm with other agencies/entities and ensure that all possible on-going projects/awarded not yet started are included therein. Check, verify and validate all the eligibility documents submitted by contractors before awarding projects.
observations.managementAction: The MPDC and BAC validated the on-going projects of the contractors and the Municipal Engineer collated the supporting documents. Liquidated damages has been computed and collected.
observations.reason: The MPDC is still collating the documents to support the extension. The BAC already validated some items; however additional documents are being collated.

observations.recommendation: We recommend that the Municipal Mayor: (a) require the Municipal Planning and Development Officer to faithfully monitor and evaluate the implementation of all funds received and to be received by the Municipality for the Supplemental Feeding Program and BUB projects and to implement the programs to avoid unnecessary delays; and (b) strictly comply with the regulations on the grant, utilization and liquidation of transfers from the national government without delays.
observations.managementAction: The MPDC is closely monitoring the implementation of all projects. The Management is trying its best to implement the programs but supply issues hindered full implementation.
observations.reason: The lack of supplier hindered the management to fully implement the project.

observations.recommendation: We recommended that the concerned officials to refund the amounts advanced for attendance to said seminar. We also recommend that Management file appropriate charges against the erring officials.
observations.managementAction: Notice of Disallowance was issued by the Audit Team to the concerned official. Settlement is on-going.
observations.reason: Other settlement is on-going and collection will still be pursued.

observations.recommendation: We recommended that the Municipal Mayor monitor the implementation of all funds received and to be received by the Municipality for the Supplemental Feeding Program to ensure that health and nutrition services are being delivered to the day care children. For the 2016 SFP funds, require the MSWDO and Bids and Awards Committee to prioritize the immediate and proper implementation of the 120-day Supplemental Feeding Program. Require the BAC to expedite the procurement process for the necessary food supplies. We also recommended that the MSWDO submit liquidation to the DSWD right after completion of the program.
observations.managementAction: The MSWDO said they prioritized health and nutrition activities and coordinated with the BAC; however supplier issues delayed procurement.
observations.reason: Supplier issues delayed the implementation; procurement constraints.

observations.recommendation: We recommended that the Municipal Mayor require the members of the Bids and Awards Committee to: • validate the tax clearance of contractors submitted and submit justification on results; • impose penalties and administrative sanctions on contractors for submission of false documents as required under RA 9184; • properly prepare bidding documents complete with accurate information and instruction to bidders; and • ensure that contracts entered in the name of the Municipality and all documents that form part of it be in accordance with the requirements of RA 9184.
observations.managementAction: The BAC checked and verified the contractors and imposed sanctions where applicable. Some contractors were required to submit additional documents and clarifications.
observations.reason: Some contractors were found with invalid documents; further actions and imposition of penalties are ongoing.

observations.recommendation: We recommended the Municipal Mayor and Municipal Accountant be prudent in checking documentary attachments. We also recommended the Management to file appropriate charges against the erring officials where applicable.
observations.managementAction: Notice of Disallowance was issued by the Audit Team to the concerned officials. The Municipal Accountant and Municipal Treasurer exercised prudence in checking documentary attachments.
observations.reason: The parties liable have not yet taken action on the Notice of Disallowance.

observations.recommendation: We recommended that the Municipal Mayor monitor the implementation of all funds received and to receive by the Municipality for the Supplemental Feeding Program to ensure that health and nutrition services are being delivered to the day care children. Require the MSWDO and Bids and Awards Committee to prioritize the immediate and proper implementation of the 120-day Supplemental Feeding Program. Require the BAC to expedite the procurement process for the necessary food supplies. We also recommended that the MSWDO submit liquidation to the DSWD right after completion of the program.
observations.managementAction: The Supplemental Feeding Program was still not implemented as of year-end. Management reported difficulties in procurement due to lack of interested suppliers.
observations.reason: The Management did not give priority to the implementation; lack of interested suppliers hindered procurement and implementation.

observations.recommendation: We recommended:  establishment of Materials Recovery Facility not only as a sorting and storage prior to collection but for recycling and composting;  include incentive based programs on its 10-year Solid Waste Management Plan as prescribed under RA 9003;  implement separate collection schedule for each type of waste, as practicable; and  dispose plastic wastes through other means possible so as not to accumulate them and cause undesirable effects in the landfill.
observations.managementAction: MRFs are not yet fully established and operational in the barangays. Materials Recovery Facilities to increase power supply and for repair of the composting drum and dump truck were being planned. Management recognized the need for incentives and programs but support from barangays was limited.
observations.reason: MRFs are not yet established; lack of support from barangay officials hindered implementation.

observations.recommendation: We recommended that the Municipal Mayor direct the Municipal Accountant to make sure that the required reconciliation are disclosed in the Notes to Financial Statements in compliance with PPSAS.
observations.managementAction: The required reconciliation was still not prepared by the Municipal Accountant.
observations.reason: The Municipality did not give priority to the implementation of the audit recommendation.

observations.recommendation:
We reiterated our recommendation that the Municipal Mayor prioritize the
completion ofphysical count of theMunicipal properties by:
(a) designating dedicated personnel under the supervision of the GSO to
continue without interruption the count of all other PPE items until
completed.
(b) requiring the designated GSO and Municipal Accountant to immediately
reconcile the results of inventory per PPE account in order to arrive at
PPE amountsthat areexisting andserviceable.
(c) compelling the use of stock cards in the General Service Office to
facilitate better monitoring and easier reconciliation.
(d) requiring execution of deeds of donation to formally transfer barangay
structures to the barangays. Consequently, we recommended the
Municipal Accountant to facilitate drawing of adjusting journal entry in
the Municipal books and the recording of said properties and costs of
their improvements in the barangay booksofaccounts.

We recommended that the Municipal Mayor require the Municipal
Treasurer to deposit collections intact daily or not later than the next working day
pursuant to Section 32 of the New GovernmentAccounting System and Section 69(1)
of Presidential Decree No. 1445. Moreover, impose strict measures on internal
control on handling collections to prevent the recurrence of lapping of Municipal
funds.

We recommended that the Municipal Mayor:
a. Instruct the Acting Municipal Treasurer to facilitate reconciliation of the
differences in the Treasury records resulting in discrepancies in the year-
enddelinquent taxes;
b. Require the Municipal Accountant to record Discounts on Real Property
Taxes-Basic/Discounts on Special Education Tax on prompt payment and
Tax Revenue-Fines and Penalties upon collection of penalties as provided
in the new chart of accounts for LGUs and reduce receivables based on
gross amounts of taxes collected and set-up receivables based on the total
collectibles fortheyear asprovided by the Municipal Treasurer; and
c. the Municipal Accountant and Municipal Treasurer to reconcile the list
of delinquent real property tax as of year-end against the recorded RPT
and SET Receivables to present an accurate and reliable receivable
accounts inthe financial statements.

We recommended that the Municipal Mayor instruct the Municipal
Accountant to record the procurement of supplies and materials for operations into
an appropriate inventory accounts and credit the same for issuance to end-users,
transfers or disposal asprovided in thenew chart ofaccounts forLGUs.

We also recommended that the Municipal Mayor require the Inventory
Committee to conduct physical count of the supplies and materials as of year-end,
prepare and submit the report of inventories and reconcile the balances with the
Accounting records to update the current balance of inventory accounts in the
financial statements.

We reiterated our recommendation that the Municipal Mayor direct the
Municipal Accountant to prepare and immediately submit the required reports and
to strictly adhere with the rules on disclosing information in the notes to financial
statements in compliance with PPSAS.

We recommended that the Municipal Accountant properly withhold
creditable income taxes of 5% or 10%, whichever is applicable, from the honoraria paid to
employees of national government agencies and remit the same to the BIR through BIR
Form No. 0605 on a monthly basis and BIR Form No. 1601-EQ on a quarterly basis, to
help the government accelerate thecollection ofincome.

We also recommended that the Municipal Accountant subject the honoraria of
employees of the municipal government to withholding tax on compensation upon
payment, in accordance with the prescribed rates under RMC No. 105-2017, and
remit thesame to theBIR through themonthly BIR Form No. 1601-C.

We recommended that the Municipal Mayor require the Municipal Engineer
and other concerned officials to furnish the Audit Team at the beginning of the year
the lists of all on-going government projects/programs/activities (“PPA”) and those
that are to be implemented during the year, and during every quarter thereafter, in
compliance with theabovecited regulations.

We recommended that the Municipal Mayor and the Honorable Members of
the Sanggunian include among their top priorities the improvement of the
Municipal storeroom for better security and organization of the accounts. We also
recommended that the Management consider planning for additional storeroom in
view of the increasing volume of financial documents and the preservation period of
ten (10) years asmandated by law.

We recommended that the Municipal Mayor require the MSWDO to include
all the eligible and qualified senior citizens in order of priority provided under the
DSWD Administrative Order No. 15 series of 2010 dated December 13, 2010 in the
updated list of eligible senior citizens for submission to the DSWD Field Office.
Likewise, conduct quarterly validation and monitoring to the beneficiaries to
determine their situation and the utilization of the stipend for the full
implementation ofSocial Pension Program ofthegovernment.

We recommended that the Municipal Accountant set-up and maintain
Special Accounts in the General Fund for the monitoring of operating performance
and viability of the Municipality’s economic enterprises, public utilities and 20%
Development Fund, and to serve as a decision making tool of the agency. Also,
record all transactions pertaining to these economic enterprises to the
corresponding subsidiary ledgers.

We recommended thatthe Management:
 Execute a yearly contract of lease embodied in a public instrument for
every public market stall rental in accordance with COA Circular No.
88-282A to ensure that interests of both parties are protected,
ambiguities and/or misunderstandings are avoided, and peaceful
operation ofthe public market in theMunicipality ismaintained.
 Formulate clear policies, rules and regulations, and administrative
provisions pertaining to collections of market stall rental fees, imposition
of surcharges, and other administrative matters affecting public market
transactions in order to attain smooth, peaceful and full control over the
conduct and operation of the public market, and to ensure that
transactions are properly documented.
 Maintain individual subsidiary ledgers of the market stall lessees for
easy monitoring andenforcing of collections.
 Effect the revision of the Local Revenue Code to attain realistic rates of
taxesand enhance collection.

We againrecommended thatthe Municipal Mayor:
 Direct the LDRRMO to incorporate in planning for the utilization of
LDRRM Fund the prior year’s unexpended balance of the LDRRMF in the
LDRRM Investment Plan to maximize all available sources ofDRRM funds.
 Instruct the LDRRMO to carefully plan disaster preparedness and
mitigation programs and activities through assessment of disaster risk in
the Municipality and its barangays. Prepare the LDRRMF Investment Plan
with emphasis on the identification of specific projects/program to be
included, the total budget available to be allotted from the current year
appropriations and the previous five years’ unexpended balance, their
proper allocation and the probability of its implementation to successfully
reduce disaster risk and mitigate the adverse effects of calamity. Determine
and update the probability and/or necessity of planned activities to keep up
with what is currently essential to the disaster-preparedness and mitigation
needs ofthe Municipality and itsbarangays.
 Limit the utilization of LDRRMF to disaster risk management activities
relative to prevention and mitigation, preparedness, response,
reconcstruction and rehabilitation, as well as procurement of basic rescue
andequipment, inaccordance with existing laws andregulations.

We recommended that the Municipal Mayor, as head of the Municipal
Development Council:
a)require the continuation of the implementation of priority developmental
projects funded by 20% Development Fund and consider other feasible
priority projects for the reversion of appropriated funds for projects not
implemented to ensure that the full intention of development will benefit all
itsconstituents;
b)impose liquidated damages to be deducted from any amount still due to the
contractor; and
c)instruct the Bids andAwards Committee to the validate the contractor’s list
of on-going projects/awarded not yet started; coordinate/confirm with
other agencies/entities and ensure that all possible on-going
projects/awarded not yet started are included therein. Check, verify and
validate all the eligibility documents submitted by contractors before
awarding projects.

Werecommended that the Municipal Mayor:
(a) require the Municipal Planning and Development Officer to faithfully
monitor and evaluate the implementation of the projects to avoid
unnecessary delays; and
(b) strictly comply with the regulations on the grant, utilization and
liquidation oftransfers from national government agencies;

We recommended that the Municipal Mayor:  create a dedicated Inventory Committee that could focus on conducting a thorough, complete and independent inventory and preparation of property cards.  Require periodic inventory report to monitor the status of their count and the submission of the annual inventory report not later than January 31 of the following year for approval, a copy furnished to the COA Auditor.  require execution of deeds of donation to formally transfer barangay structures to the barangays. Consequently, we recommended the Municipal Accountant to facilitate drawing of adjusting journal entry in the Municipal books and the recording of said properties and costs of their improvements in the barangay books of accounts.
observations.managementAction: The designated General Services Officer-Designate, who is the appointed Chairman of the Inventory Committee, disclosed that they were not able to conduct physical inventory of all inventory accounts due to lack of manpower resulting in non-preparation of the Report on the Physical Count of Inventory (RPCI) as of year-end contrary with the abovementioned rules and regulations, hence, the existence of the account balance was notestablished. The Due to the absence of a permanent GSO and lack of staff a Inventory Committee is to be created as recommended, and Management promised to temporarily re-assign Job Order personnel from other offices to assist the designated GSO in inventory taking. The Management also acknowledged the importance of property management to ease up updating and reconciling inventory results with that ofaccounting records.
observations.reason: The physical count of all Municipal properties was not completed due to lack of dedicated staff to perform the count.

observations.recommendation: We recommended that the Municipal Mayor:  Instruct the Acting Municipal Treasurer to facilitate reconciliation of the differences in the Treasury records resulting in discrepancies in the year-end delinquent taxes;  Require the Municipal Accountant to record Discounts on Real Property Taxes-Basic/Discounts on Special Education Tax on prompt payment and Tax Revenue-Fines and Penalties upon collection of penalties as provided in the new chart of accounts for LGUs and reduce receivables based on gross amounts of taxes collected and set-up receivables based on the total collectibles for the year as provided by the Municipal Treasurer; and  the Municipal Accountant and Municipal Treasurer to reconcile the list of delinquent real property tax as of year-end against the recorded RPT and SET Receivables to present an accurate and reliable receivable accounts in the financial statements.
observations.managementAction: The Management agreed to comply with the recommendations and will verify and reconcile records of the Municipal Treasurer, Municipal Assessor and Municipal Accountant. The Municipal Accountant acknowledged and agreed to comply with the recommendations on proper recording of discounts, fines and penalties starting 2018 transactions.
observations.reason: The Municipality did not give priority to the implementation of the audit recommendation; hence, reiteration of this finding in Part II of the report.

observations.recommendation: We recommended that the Municipal Treasurer and the Municipal Accountant make proper coordination with the concerned bank officials to determine the reason for the existence of the reconciling items and effect adjustment in the books of accounts. Furthermore, ensure that the required reconciliations are effected and the necessary adjustments undertaken so that the cash in bank balance presented in the financial statements is accurate and reliable.
observations.managementAction: Stale checks were reverted to Accounts Payable, while other reconciling items are still being verified by the Municipal Accountant. Reconciliation of the Treasury and Accounting Units were undertaken in CY2018.
observations.reason: Cash books were not updated on a timely basis which hindered the periodic reconciliation of the Treasury and Accounting Units in CY2017.

observations.recommendation: We recommended that the Municipal Mayor: a. maximize the appropriations of the 20% Development Fund; b. coordinate with the National Agencies, Province and Barangay which may have projects for implementation that might coincide with the planned project of the Municipality; c. carefully plot the timing of implementation with the availability of IRA; and d. have proper coordination among the project beneficiaries and the officials involved in planning, execution and monitoring of the projects. We also recommended that Management cause the immediate implementation of the projects that had been allocated funds in order to fully attain the purpose and intent for establishing the fund at the soonest possible time, thereby, maximizing the real value of the money on hand.
observations.managementAction: The Municipal Mayor appropriate efforts to fully attain the purpose and intent in establishing the 20% Development Fund. Meanwhile, only ₱8,383,115.26 or 56.33% out of the ₱14,881,845.26 of utilization was utilized under the current year’s appropriation. Management will coordinate and validate projects and ensure feasibility before inclusion.
observations.reason: Balances of continuing appropriations (2016 and below) as of December 31, 2017 was not provided to the Audit Team, hence, validation of utilization could not be performed.

observations.recommendation: We recommended that the Municipal Chief Executive instruct the Municipal Accountant to: Demand letters to be immediately issued to officials with unliquidated cash advances; allow only those duly appointed or designated disbursing officers perform disbursing functions; cause the withholding of salary of the Accountable Officers (AOs) who failed to settle/liquidate their cash advances despite the issuance of demand letters in accordance with Section 5.9 of COA Circular No. 97-002; and exercise utmost diligence in monitoring liquidation of cash advances.
observations.managementAction: Noted that demand letters were immediately issued with some compliance but liquidation of some cash advances was still observed due to non-compliance of some officials with unliquidated cash advances. The Municipal Accountant issued demand letters to concerned officials.
observations.reason: Delayed liquidation of some cash advances was still observed due to non-compliance of officials despite issuance of demand letters.

observations.recommendation: We recommended that the Municipal Mayor instruct the Municipal Treasurer and the Municipal Accountant to effect payment for gasoline/fuel consumption directly to supplier and not on reimbursement basis for control purposes and also to ensure payment of appropriate taxes due the government. Likewise, direct all drivers of the Municipality’s motor vehicles to prepare Driver’s Trip Tickets serially numbered and properly accomplish odometer readings before and after each trip. Also, to direct the General Services Office to comply with the preparation and submission to COA of the monthly reports pertaining to official travels and fuel consumption to enable the Audit Team to evaluate the reasonableness of fuel consumption.
observations.managementAction: Payments for gasoline/fuel consumption were made directly to supplier during the year. The Report of official travels and fuel consumption was prepared, however these were still not submitted on a monthly basis to the Audit Team. Driver's Trip Tickets remained not serially numbered, and majority did not indicate the odometer reading before and after each trip.
observations.reason: Deficiencies in controls over fuel consumption persisted.

observations.recommendation: We recommended that the Municipal Mayor:  designate more staff to enable performance of the LDRRM functions;  prepare the LDRRMO Investment Plan with emphasis on identification of specific projects/program to be included, the total budget available to be allotted from the current year appropriations and the previous five years’ unexpended balance, their proper allocation and probability of implementation; and  limit the utilization of LDRRMF to disaster risk management activities relative to prevention and mitigation, preparedness, response, reconstruction and rehabilitation, as well as procurement of basic rescue and equipment in accordance with existing laws and regulations.
observations.managementAction: Two staff and three Job Order personnel were assigned to the LDRRMO to assist in the performance of the LDRRM functions. However, the LDRRMO Investment Plan was still not prepared in accordance with regulations, leading to non-utilization of available funds.
observations.reason: Deficiencies in planning leading to non-utilization of available funds; LDRRMO Investment Plan was not prepared in accordance with regulations.

observations.recommendation: We recommended that:  the Management appropriate at least 5% of total CY 2017 appropriations for GAD as required under pertinent regulations;  the TWG conduct proper survey and study as guided by PCW-DILG-DBM-NEDA Joint Memorandum Circular (JMC) No. 2013-01 to determine gender issues surfacing in the locality and perform gender mainstreaming of the activities of the Municipality and its barangays;  the TWG prepare a GAD Plan based on established gender issues specifying the objectives and funds allocated to each activity and submit the same to DILG for review and approval;  formulate a GAD Code and set up and maintain a GAD database that will serve as basis for gender-responsive planning, programming and policy formulation; and  ensure proper implementation of GAD related projects incorporated in the DILG approved GAD Plan and Budget.
observations.managementAction: The Management appropriated 5.21% of the budget or ₱3,900,500.00 for GAD; of which ₱1,353,491.00 was utilized. The GAD Plan and Budget for CY 2017 was submitted to the Audit Team. GFPS TWG are in the process of formulating a Gender and Development Plan and GAD Code. However, gender disaggregated data and full implementation of gender sensitive activities remain incomplete.
observations.reason: GAD activities embodied in the plan are not gender responsive/sensitive and do not address client-based gender issues identified through documented information and sex-disaggregated data.

observations.recommendation: We recommended that the Municipal Mayor require the Municipal Engineer and the Municipal Planning and Development Coordinator to ensure posting of public notice on all infrastructure projects to promote public awareness on the use of government funds and to inform the Audit Team that the posting is already done in the site and in conspicuous places within the agency premises.
observations.managementAction: Annual dues were posted in the site in conspicuous places within the agency premises.

observations.recommendation: We recommended strict observance of provisions of Article 210 to 213 and 215 of RA 7160 and Section 3.4 of National Budget Circular No. 563 and that contributions and payments to non-sanctioned leagues not be charged against municipality funds.
observations.managementAction: The Municipality paid annual dues to leagues provided in Article 210 to 213 and 215 of RA 7160. Contributions and payments to non-sanctioned leagues were not charged against municipality funds.

observations.recommendation: We recommended the:  Bids and Awards Committee to check, verify and validate all the tax clearance certificates submitted by the contractors awarded contracts by the Municipal Government and submit a report on the results thereof;  Impose penalties and administrative sanctions on the submission of false documents as required of Section 69.1(a)(b), Rule XXIII of RA 9184;  BAC properly prepare the bidding documents, complete with accurate information and instruction to bidders; and  Management ensure that contracts entered in the name of the Municipality as the procuring entity and all documents that form part of it be in accordance with the requirements of RA 9184.
observations.managementAction: The Bids and Awards Committee was advised to validate the tax certificates submitted by the contractors. However, justification was not submitted by the BAC and penalties were not imposed.
observations.reason: Justification was not submitted by the BAC, and penalties were not imposed on the contractors.

observations.recommendation: conduct complete inventory of the Municipality’s properties, in view of the large amount of recorded PPE that were excluded in the inventory, and coordinate with the Municipal Accountant to reconcile their records in order to establish the differences in the cost and quantity and condition of the PPE reflected in the financial statements;
observations.managementAction: Management was not able to conduct complete inventory of the properties. Refer to finding no. 1 in Part II of this report.
observations.reason: Multiple designation to the GSO compromised his time and ability to focus on the conduct of a complete inventory of the properties throughout the year since he is a staff also of the Municipal Agriculturist Officeand the sole person responsible for the general services of the Municipality which involves property management (custody and monitoring) and procurement procedures from canvass to the preparation of purchase orders and other relevant documents.

observations.recommendation: locate for the KIA Besta Ambulance (SDS 904), Nissan LMP-DILG; and Back – Up Type, Diesel, 4 wheel drive. If found missing, hold the person accountable thereof liable and require payment of the cost of the lost property or may request relief from accountability to the COA.
observations.managementAction: The management cannot locate the Service Vehicles mentioned.
observations.reason: Recommendation is no longer doable.

observations.recommendation: secure custody of the unserviceable owner type jeep (SDW 289) located in the repair shop so as not to lose more of its parts.
observations.managementAction: The management cannot locate the Service Vehicle mentioned.
observations.reason: Recommendation is no longer doable.

observations.recommendation: reconcile with the designated GSO the discrepancies noted in values and quantities of PPE items;
observations.managementAction: Reconciliation was not performed since inventory taking was not conducted in time with the closing of financial statements.
observations.reason: Lack of manpower to conduct thorough inventory taking throughout the year

observations.recommendation: verify the remaining useful life of unserviceable properties. Recognize depreciation expense as of December 31, 2014 as Prior Period Adjustments for those that are still depreciable; and provide depreciation expense for PPE items that were not depreciated.
observations.managementAction: The management looked for the records of the “PPE not depreciated” items as listed in the schedule.
observations.reason: Complete records relative to the PPE items not depreciated were not secured as basis for the computation of depreciation.

observations.recommendation: We recommended that the Management submit the basis/guidelines for the grant of uniform amount of ₱25,000 to officials and employees. Otherwise, the total amount will be disallowed in audit.
observations.managementAction: No basis/guidelines for the grant of uniform amount of ₱25,000 PBB was submitted, hence Notice of Disallowance No. 2016-001-100(2015) dated August 16, 2016 was issued on January 12, 2017. The Management submitted their appeal on May 15, 2017.
observations.reason: Refund is not enforced until a decision is rendered on the appeal filed.

observations.recommendation: We recommended that the rate of implementation of projects funded from the 20% Development Fund be enhanced through closer coordination among the officials involved in planning, execution and monitoring and the employment of regular monitoring mechanism in assessing the progress of project implementation in order to deliver to constituents the desired services and benefits which go with these projects.
observations.managementAction: The Management was not able to utilize its 20% Development Fund as only 21 projects with total cost of ₱9,904,787.59 have been implemented during the year while eight projects were not undertaken despite the existence of appropriations of ₱1,326,328.00 and availability of funds for the purpose. Refer to finding number 4 in Part II of this report.
observations.reason: Projects not implemented were due to reasons distinct for every project such as improper planning, heavy rains, problems with ownership, simultaneous projects implemented by the barangay, provincial and national government.

observations.recommendation: ensure that cash advances are liquidated within 30 days after its purpose has been served.
observations.managementAction: As of December 31, 2016, ₱7,100.00 remained unliquidated and has been outstanding for three months and two week as of year-end. Refer to findings number 5 on Part II of this report.
observations.reason: Despite demand by the Municipal Accounting Office, the accountable officer did not readily liquidate his overdue cash advance.

observations.recommendation: We recommended that the municipality strictly implement its Environmental Code in terms of ensuring cleanliness, proper waste segregation at source in the establishment, market and households;
observations.managementAction: Proper waste segregation was still not fully implemented and monitored especially in barangays.
observations.reason: Lack of cooperation of households

observations.recommendation: In order to effectively implement gender activities, we recommended that: members of the GFPS, as spearheaded by the MSWDO, conduct examination of the agency’s level of gender mainstreaming or extent of the gender responsiveness of the Municipal programs and activities, the level of gender awareness and competence of its personnel and the presence or absence of enabling mechanisms that support gender mainstreaming.
observations.managementAction: The GFPS was not able to conduct gender survey to properly identify gender issues in the Municipality. Refer to finding number 11 in Part II of this report.
observations.reason: As these are additional functions to the GFPS, they could not readily focus on their duties for gender and development implementation in the Municipality.

observations.recommendation: the GFPS determine programs, projects and activities that are responsive to the gender gaps or issues faced by their women and men employees, as well as their clients and constituents.
observations.managementAction: The GFPS were not able to properly identify gender issues in the Municipality.
observations.reason: They lack training and thorough orientation on how to properly identify gender issues.

observations.recommendation: the TWG prepare a GAD Plan based on established gender issues, specify the cause and objective and the funds allocated to finance each and every activity.
observations.managementAction: The approved GAD Plan still presented GAD activities that could not be considered   gender related based on the specified gender issues meant to be addressed.
observations.reason: The GFPS lack training and thorough orientation on how to properly identify gender issues.

observations.recommendation: the TWG prepare the consolidated GAD Accomplishment Report (AR) based from the DILG-approved GPB following the instructions and format prescribed under the aforementioned Joint Circular to provide consistent data for more accurate assessment of actual accomplishments.
observations.managementAction: GAD Accomplishment Report was prepared but it was not based on the approved GAD Plan and Budget because the GAD Plan and Budget of the Municipality was submitted late to the Provincial Planning and Development Office and was not forwarded to the Department of Interior and Local Government (DILG); thus no Certificate of Approval was received by the Municipality.
observations.reason: Preparation of GAD Plan and Budget was not prioritized.

observations.recommendation: MDRRMO carefully plan disaster preparedness and mitigation programs and activities and ensure implementation thereof in order to maximize the LDRRM Fund;
observations.managementAction: The Municipal LDRRMO planned activities that were the same as last year’s plan and presented them in generic. Refer to finding number 9 in Part II of this report.
observations.reason: Lack of focus and comprehensive understanding and evaluation of disaster needs of the Municipality

observations.recommendation: MDRRMO update annually the list of projects and activities to be funded from LDRRMF specially on the unexpended QRF and DRRM – MOOE of the previous years transferred to Special Trust Fund;
observations.managementAction: Same as above
observations.reason: Lack of focus and comprehensive understanding and evaluation of disaster needs of the Municipality

observations.recommendation: Municipal Planning and Development Officer coordinate with the designated LDRMMO to ensure that budgets per LDRRMFIP are consistent with that of the Annual Investment Plan;
observations.managementAction: Same as above
observations.reason: Lack of focus and comprehensive understanding and evaluation of disaster needs of the Municipality

observations.recommendation: MDRRMO implement projects and activities in the LDRRMFIP specially activities and projects under the Prevention and Mitigation and Preparedness.
observations.managementAction: Disaster preparedness and mitigating activities undertaken during the year were limited. Refer to finding number 9 in Part II of this report.
observations.reason: Miscommunication and confusions during planning resulted in having planned activities for one and the same fund. Implementation was also not given proper attention.

observations.recommendation: Municipal Accountant disclose in the Notes to Financial Statements the amount and details of the unexpended balance of LDRRMF in compliance to Section 5.1.16 of Commission on Audit Circular No. 2012-002;
observations.managementAction: The Municipal Accounting Office did not provide in the 2016 Notes to Financial Statements the details of the unexpended LDRRMF balance from previous years.
observations.reason: A schedule for the unexpended balance was rather provided.

observations.recommendation: We recommended that the Municipal Mayor require its officials and employees to refund the PEI received in excess of the ₱5,000.00 limit set under DBM Budget Circular No. 2014-3 dated December 2, 2014.
observations.managementAction: The Municipal officials and employees have filed an appeal to the Office of the COA Regional Director.
observations.reason: Appeal is yet to be decided by the Office of the Regional Director.

observations.recommendation:
We recommended that the Municipal Mayor:
create a dedicated Inventory Committee that could focus on conducting a thorough, complete and independent physical count of all Municipal real properties and semi-expendable items and preparation of property cards. Require periodic inventory report to monitor the status of their count and the submission of the annual inventory report not later than January 31 of the following year for approval, a copy furnished to the COA Auditor.
direct the Inventory Committee to maintain PPE property cards, and reconcile results of inventory with the accounting records to arrive at the proper balance of the PPE accounts. Any unserviceable properties should be counted separately and for disposal periodic reconciliation of these records with subsidy ledgers of the Municipal Accountant.

We recommended that the Municipal Mayor require execution of deeds of donation to formally transfer barangay structures to the barangays. Consequently, we recommended the Municipal Accountant to facilitate drawing of adjusting journal entry in the Municipal books and the recording of said properties and costs of their improvements in the barangay books of accounts.

We recommended that the Municipal Accountant and the Municipal Treasurer cause immediate reconciliation of records to reflect reliable account balances. Moreover, ensure that appropriate actions could be undertaken to recover such collectibles.

We recommended that the Municipal Treasurer and the Municipal Accountant cause reconciliation of records for more reliable account balances and the Municipal Accountant make proper coordination with the concerned bank officials to determine the reason for the existence of the reconciling items and effect adjustment in the books of who committed the error or discrepancy.  Furthermore, effect the necessary adjustments of reconciling items determined during the preparation of the Monthly Bank Reconciliation Statements on a timely basis for an accurate and reliable Cash in Bank account balance in the financial statements.

We recommended that the Municipal Mayor require the concerned officials to refund the amounts advanced for attendance to said seminar. We also recommend the Management to file appropriate charges against the erring officials.
We further recommended that the Municipal Accountant and Municipal Treasurer be prudent in checking documentary attachments submitted to their office.

We recommended that the Municipal Mayor instruct the Municipal Treasurer and the Municipal Accountant to effect payment for gasoline/fuel consumption directly to supplier and not on reimbursement basis for control purposes and also to ensure payment of appropriate taxes due the government.
Likewise, we recommended that the Municipal Mayor require the General Services Officer to instruct all drivers of the Municipality’s motor vehicles to report defective odometer/speedometer so that the necessary repair can be undertaken and to properly accomplish the Driver’s Trip Tickets which should be serially numbered for control purposes.  Also, to direct the General Services Office to comply with the preparation and submission to COA of the monthly reports pertaining to official travels and fuel consumption to enable the Audit Team to evaluate the reasonableness of fuel consumption.

We recommended that the Municipal Mayor monitor the implementation of all funds received and to be received by the Municipality for the Supplemental Feeding Program to ensure that health and nutrition services are being delivered to the day care children. For the 2016 SFP funds, require the MSWDO and Bids and Awards Committee to prioritize the immediate and proper implementation of the 120-day Supplemental Feeding Program.  Require the BAC to expedite the procurement process for the necessary food supplies.  We also recommended that the MSWDO submit liquidation to the DSWD right after completion of the program.

We recommended that the:
Municipal Mayor designate more staff to enable proper performance of the LDRRM functions; and
MDRRMO carefully plan disaster preparedness and mitigation programs and activities through assessment of disaster risk in the Municipality and its barangays. Prepare the LDRRMF Investment Plan with emphasis on the identification of specific projects/program to be included, the total budget available to be allotted from the current year appropriations and the previous five years’ unexpended balance, their proper allocation and the probability of its implementation to successfully reduce disaster risk and mitigate the adverse effects of calamity. Determine and update the probability and/or necessity of planned activities to keep up with what is currently essential to the disaster-preparedness and mitigation needs of the Municipality and its barangays.

We recommended that the Municipal Mayor require the Municipal Engineer and the Municipal Planning and Development Coordinator to ensure posting of public notice on all infrastructure projects to promote public awareness on the use of government funds and to inform the Audit Team that the posting is already done in the site and in conspicuous places within the agency premises.

We recommended that the Municipality strictly observe provisions of Article 210 to 213 and 215 of RA 7160 and Section 3.4 of National Budget Circular No. 563April 22, 2016.  Contributions and elective officials’ expenses related to leagues which are not included in the preceding articles are not government expenses and should not be charged against the funds of the municipality.
We recommended that the Municipal Mayor direct the Municipal Accountant to make sure that the required reconciliation are disclosed in the Notes to Financial Statements in compliance with PPSAS.
renew the Memorandum Receipt for properties during change of end-user or transfer from one office to another for proper identification and location of PPE items.
observations.managementAction: Memonradum receipts were issued and monitored during transfer of accountability.

observations.recommendation: provide a supporting schedule of the unreconciled amount in the Land account. Also, recognize the parcels of land in their acquisition cost if acquired through purchase,  and in their fair values at the date of acquisition if acquired at no or nominal cost;
observations.managementAction: The Management was not able to finalize reconciliation of the Land account.
observations.reason: The Management is still looking for the copies of deed of donation of donated lands and copy of tax declaration from the Assessor’s Office to get the values of the said lands as basis of recording.

observations.recommendation: prepare a PPE schedule with complete details as to the economic useful life (EUL), remaining useful life (RUL);
observations.managementAction: The Municipal GSO and Accountant was still not able to arrive at a reconciled balance of the PPE accounts.
observations.reason: There are items in the PPE schedule that has no record on the accounting office.

observations.recommendation: We recommended the following courses of action:
observations.managementAction: The management released all unpaid retirement benefits due to all retiring officials and employees as of December 31, 2016.

observations.recommendation: that the Acting HRMO and Municipal Budget Officer ensure that all terminal leave benefits already due to retired officials and employees as of December 31, 2015 have corresponding appropriation to facilitate full payment thereof;
observations.managementAction: The Acting HRMO and Municipal Budget Officer ensured that all terminal leave benefits due to retired officials and employees as of December 31, 2016 have corresponding appropriation.

observations.recommendation: that the Municipal Budget Officer refrain from realigning allotments for the payment of terminal leave benefits until all terminal leave benefits due and payable are paid;
observations.managementAction: Allotments for the payment of terminal leave benefits are not realigned until all terminal leave benefits due and payable are paid.

observations.recommendation: that the Municipal Treasurer and Municipal Mayor prioritize the payments of Terminal Leave Benefits when there are savings from other Personal Services allotments.
observations.managementAction: As of year-end, Terminal Leave Benefits were paid when due.

observations.recommendation: We recommended that the Municipal Mayor instruct the General Services Officer-Designate to meticulously and judiciously plan procurement for each quarter as consolidated and incorporate them in the Annual Procurement Plan (APP) in order to adopt competitive/public bidding to provide reasonable assurance that the best quality at the lowest possible price is availed. Ensure that only those that are emergency in nature and those ordinary or regular supplies and materials not available in the Procurement Services (PS) and within the threshold are purchased through shopping.
observations.managementAction: Procurement of IT Equipment, supplies and materials and other needs of various departments were being consolidated.

observations.recommendation: We recommended that the disbursements from special accounts shall proceed from itemized appropriation which shall be for specific development projects/activities embodied in the local development plan and/or public investment program formulated and prioritized by the Local Development Council and approved by the Sanggunian concerned.
observations.managementAction: The projects embodied in the Annual Investment Program were now itemized.

observations.recommendation: We recommended that the Municipal Accountant: immediately effect the withholding of salary of the concerned official with unliquidated cash advance; ensure that cash advances are liquidated within 30 days after its purpose has been served. For failure of the accountable officers to settle/liquidate outstanding accountabilities as soon as the purpose for which it was given has been served, send demand letters enjoining the officials and employees concerned. If demands were unheeded, suspension of payment of salaries until the outstanding balance of accountabilities are fully settled shall be enforced as provided in COA Circular No. 2012-004; and refrain from processing disbursement vouchers for a new cash advance of accountable officers until their previous cash advance has been settled. Exercise utmost diligence in monitoring liquidation of cash advances.
observations.managementAction: The concerned employee had already liquidated the cash advance mentioned.

observations.recommendation: refrain from processing disbursement vouchers for a new cash advance of accountable officers until their previous cash advance has been settled.
observations.managementAction: No cash advances were granted until previous cash advances were liquidated.

observations.recommendation: We also recommended that the Municipal Mayor require that only the duly appointed or designated disbursing officers perform disbursing functions in accordance with Section 4.1.5 COA Circular No. 97-002. Require also the Municipal Accountant to grant cash advances by individual and not as a group.
observations.managementAction: During CY 2016, cash advances were granted by individual. However, there were still grants to non—designated disbursing officers. Refer to findings number 2 on Part II of this report.
observations.reason: Exigency of expenses called for the immediate grant of cash advances to special disbursing officers even without the expressed designation from the Municipal Mayor.

observations.recommendation: the MENRO ensure that all Municipal and Barangay garbage collectors wear proper protective equipment when handling solid wastes;
observations.managementAction: Proper protective materials such as gloves and masks were provided to garbage collectors.

observations.recommendation: management include in the Project Procurement Management Plan the purchase of the equipment/machineries necessary to effectively implement the SWM so it may be included in the Annual Procurement Plan and in the budget.
observations.managementAction: Although compartments were not provided in the truck, residual and biodegradable wastes are arranged separately during hauling.
observations.reason: Funds for the purchase of brand new hammer mill is being sought from provincial and/or national government.

observations.recommendation: management device a scheduling system for collection of different solid waste classification or restructure the vehicle used in garbage collection so that different solid wastes will have proper compartments and cover;
observations.managementAction: Only one composting drum, one dump truck and 12 tri-bikes are still in use at present. Serviceable shredders and plastic oven were not often used due to insufficiency of electric power supply at the site.
observations.reason: Having such programs was not given proper attention by the Solid Waste Management Officer due to lack of staff to facilitate implementation and monitoring of waste management among barangays.

observations.recommendation: Municipal Mayor provide for the necessary and complete personnel under the LDRRMO so that the LDRRM functions could be performed well; and
observations.managementAction: On the latter part of the year, the Municipal Mayor assigned a full time LDRRMO with two staffs.
observations.reason: As the planning was not given proper attention, implementation was compromised.

observations.recommendation: Municipal Budget Officer maintain a Registry of Appropriations, Allotments and Obligations (RAAO) for LDRRMF as follows: a. RAAO for QRF, b. RAAO for MF - MOOE and c. RAAO for MF – CO;
observations.managementAction: RAAO for is already being maintained for LDRRMF as follows: a. RAAO for QRF, b. RAAO for MF - MOOE and c. RAAO for MF – CO.

observations.recommendation: Municipal Accountant prepare the Monthly Reports on Sources and utilization of DRRMF and the LDRRMFOfficer submit through the LDRRMC and Local Development Council to the COA Auditor of the LGU, copy furnished the Office of the Civil Defense and the DILG – Local Government Operations Office;
observations.managementAction: The Municipal Accountant prepared the Monthly Reports on Sources and utilization of DRRMF and submitted it to the COA Auditor.

observations.recommendation: General Services Officer issue an acknowledgement receipt to the donor for the received fire truck; and
observations.managementAction: Fire truck was acknowledged through the contract and physical delivery. Fair value of the fire truck donated was not recorded in the books.
observations.reason: Since it was already donated and ownership has been transferred to the Bureau of Fire Protection (BFP), recording the asset and derecognizing it afterwards would have no effect in the Government Equity account.

observations.recommendation: Municipal Accountant draw an adjusting entry for the recognition in the Municipal books of the fair value of the fire truck received and provide depreciation thereof.
observations.managementAction: Fair value of the fire truck donated was not recorded in the books.
observations.reason: Since it was already donated and ownership has been transferred to the Bureau of Fire Protection (BFP), recording the asset and derecognizing it afterwards would have no effect in the Government Equity account.
